input_path: data\papers\Ramdhan et al. - 2025 - Engineering Automotive Digital Twins on Standardized Architectures A Case Study.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The physical twin is a 1/10‑scale teleoperated autonomous vehicle retrofitted with advanced sensors and control hardware, comprising lower and upper chassis levels and dedicated autonomy elements (e.g., perception, planning, actuation modules). It operates in a vehicle‑centric domain where resource‑constrained ECUs run Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) functions, making it a suitable candidate for off‑loading computation and remote management via a digital twin.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin delivers three core services: (1) Data Monitoring – a graphical UI streams real‑time vehicle state, projected driving path, and nearby object tracks, and supports data viewing, saving, and report generation for operators and developers; (2) Computation Offloading – the ACC algorithm (lead‑vehicle detection, desired‑speed calculation) is executed on the DT’s cloud resources, with the resulting speed command transmitted back to the vehicle for actuation; (3) Remote Management – the UI enables operators to enable/disable ACC, set desired speed, and issue emergency‑brake commands, providing low‑latency, safety‑critical control from a remote location.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: Implementation relies on a middleware layer that connects the vehicle to the DT over a low‑latency communication link, cloud computing infrastructure that hosts the offloaded ACC logic, and a custom user‑interface application that aggregates monitoring visualizations and control commands. The DT system (DTS) incorporates the DT entity, physical and middleware components, and the UI to satisfy data collection, reporting, and real‑time interaction requirements.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The system requires a shared notion of time among devices to support computation offloading. Clock synchronization must achieve at least 100 ms accuracy, which is the imposed limit due to hardware constraints and conforms to safety‑critical vehicular standards. No finer granularity or latency figures are provided.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The architecture follows the ISO 23247 reference model and defines three top‑level entities – Physical Twin Entity, Digital Twin Entity, and User Entity – each instantiated on separate hardware components. Each entity contains one or more Functional Entities (e.g., User Interface FE, Data Storage FE). The documents do not describe multiple parallel twin instances, centralized versus decentralized deployment, or hierarchical coordination beyond this three‑entity split.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The Digital Twin system is orchestrated according to the ISO 23247 reference architecture. Software components are identified, mapped to Functional Entities, and each Functional Entity is implemented as an individual ROS 2 package containing one or more ROS 2 nodes. Entities are deployed on distinct compute resources (e.g., separate hardware for Physical Twin, Digital Twin, and User entities), providing a distributed, entity‑based orchestration. Coordination relies on the entity relationships defined in ISO 23247 and on ROS 2 communication mechanisms.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: The documents reference ISO 23247 Part 4 (Information exchange) and note that Functional Entities may be realized using standards such as OPC Unified Architecture, but no concrete data‑exchange protocol, interoperability layer, or integration with external enterprise systems is described. Hence, specific horizontal integration details are not provided.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin follows the ISO 23247 reference architecture and organises its software into ROS 2 packages, each implementing a Functional Entity. Within the Digital Twin Entity, data‑management functionality is provided by a Data Storage component and other control mechanisms. The documents do not specify concrete computational model types (geometric, behavioral, analytical) nor detailed data source or management approaches beyond this ROS‑based implementation.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Actuation capabilities are placed in the Physical Twin Entity, which hosts device‑communication and actuation functionality implemented as ROS 2 nodes within Functional Entities. No specific actuator types, control ranges, safety constraints, or remote interface details are given in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Sensing resources are also part of the Physical Twin Entity. Sensors provide data that feed preprocessing, point‑cloud clustering, and tracking functions, realised as ROS 2 nodes. The documents do not furnish details on sensor types, measurement specifications, sampling frequencies, transmission rates, or accuracy.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: The architecture mandates a shared notion of time across devices, requiring clock‑synchronisation accuracy of 100 ms for safety‑critical vehicular applications, which constitutes a fidelity requirement for data exchange and control. No additional model validation, verification, uncertainty quantification, or error‑correction procedures are described.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Sensor data from the physical twin (LiDAR point‑cloud, motor state, steering servo position, overall vehicle state) is first captured by the Physical Twin Entity. Raw LiDAR points are processed by the Data Pre‑Processing Functional Entity (Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking) which converts individual 2‑D point samples into tracked object detections. All data streams are then handed to the ISO 23247 Data Translation FE, which serialises each stream, aligns message types and performs required unit conversions. Within the Digital Twin System the processed streams are exchanged between software components via a ROS 2 network (publish/subscribe topics). Communication that crosses entity boundaries (Physical Twin ↔ Digital Twin ↔ User Entity) uses a Wi‑Fi network with UDP transport. The resulting vehicle‑state, object‑track and projected‑path information is fed in real‑time to the 3D Visualization and Presentation FE for operator monitoring.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: The Digital Twin Entity generates control commands for the physical twin through its Controlling FE (ACC). Commands include enable/disable ACC, set‑desired‑speed, emergency‑brake, and the computed desired speed that the vehicle must actuate. These commands are passed to the Data Translation FE for serialisation, message‑type alignment and unit conversion, then transmitted over the Wi‑Fi UDP link to the Physical Twin Entity. The Actuation FE on the physical side receives the commands and drives the motor and steering servo actuators. Vehicle‑state feedback is continuously streamed back via the same data‑translation pipeline, providing confirmation of command execution. The documents do not detail explicit validation or authentication mechanisms, but the system is described as safety‑critical and must meet low‑latency requirements.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The Digital Twin System uses a mixed network architecture. Inside each hardware platform, all functional entities are implemented as ROS 2 packages; each package contains one or more ROS 2 nodes that communicate over ROS 2 topics for intra‑entity data exchange. Across entities (Physical Twin, Digital Twin, User Entity) communication is performed over a Wi‑Fi network using UDP at the transport layer. Clock synchronisation across devices is required; standards for safety‑critical vehicular applications demand sub‑100 ms accuracy, and the system targets a 100 ms synchronisation tolerance. The DT must provide low end‑to‑end latency for ACC operation, though exact latency budgets are not quantified. No explicit bandwidth, redundancy, or security protocols are mentioned in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The digital twin system is built on the ISO 23247 standard for manufacturing digital twins. It follows Part 2 of the standard, which defines an entity‑based reference architecture (Data Collection and Device Control Entity, Digital Twin Entity, User Entity, and Observable Manufacturing Element). The design explicitly maps software components to Functional Entities as prescribed by the ISO 23247 RA. OPC Unified Architecture is cited as an example protocol that can be used to implement data exchange between functional entities, reflecting compliance with the standard’s recommended communication mechanisms. The implementation acknowledges deviations (e.g., introduction of a Physical Twin Entity) but remains anchored to the ISO 23247 framework, indicating intentional alignment with that industry standard.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations: The case study reports that the ISO 23247 reference architecture (RA) does not address safety‑critical requirements for automotive digital twins. It recommends elevating safety to a first‑class concern by integrating the functional‑safety standard ISO 26262 (ISO 26262‑1:2018) into the DT design. Safety analysis, risk assessment and fail‑safe mechanisms defined in ISO 26262 should be applied to the DT components (e.g., Data Pre‑Processing FE, Actuation FE) deployed on the physical twin. The authors note that existing automotive standards such as AUTOSAR provide hardware‑agnostic component definitions that can be reused within the DT to support safety‑critical deployments.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
_A. Context_


DT technology enables an array of benefits for ACC through
off-loading any critical or non-critical computation for which
resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient. To implement the ACC-DT, we develop
the following three services through a DT.
_1) Data Monitoring:_ Monitoring ACC-relevant data flowing through a vehicle is crucial for tracking system performance. The DT presented in this case will contain a user
interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate
reports of vehicle state data. Additionally, the UI will display
real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and
detected object tracks in the vehicle’s proximity.
_2) Computation Offloading:_ A DT can alleviate the computational burden placed on resource-constrained on-board
hardware by allocating compute to better-suited equipment,
e.g., in the cloud. In this case study, we demonstrate this
functionality by offloading only the ACC system from the
vehicle to the DT. The ACC system determines the lead
vehicle, then computes a desired speed based on the distance
to the lead vehicle, finally sending the desired speed back to
the vehicle where the actuation is carried out.


(a) Base hardware: teleoperated vehicle retrofitted with advanced
sensor and control technology



(b) Main hardware layers of the physical twin: lower and upper level
chassis, and the autonomy elements



Fig. 1: The physical twin: a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle



_3) Remote Management:_ The ability to remotely manage
ADAS systems enables the development, testing, and operation
of these systems from a different geographical location than
the physical twin. In addition to displaying real-time state data,
the UI will also enable the management and operation of ACC
remotely. This includes enabling/disabling ACC, setting the
desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command.
To implement these services, the DT must support low
end-to-end latency communication with the vehicle, contain
data collection and reporting capabilities, and more—all while
respecting the safety-critical nature of the system. For convenience and following community best practices, we refer
to the collection of the DT Entity, physical and middleware
components, and the UI as the digital twin system (DTS).
The users of our system can be grouped into two classes:
operators and developers. Operators use the DT to monitor
and remotely manage ACC, and developers use the DT to
troubleshoot during development using the data collection and
monitoring capabilities. In the _Liberty to act vs. Ability to act_
taxonomy from David and Bork [30], this DTS falls under the
Fully Autonomous Digital Twin, with the Human-in-the-Loop.


_B. Physical Twin_

coordination between devices. To enable computation
offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.


**Physical Twin Entity*** **Digital Twin Entity** **User Entity**

In addition, a demonstration of the DT is publicly available. [2]


II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.

_A. Context_


DT technology enables an array of benefits for ACC through
off-loading any critical or non-critical computation for which
resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient. To implement the ACC-DT, we develop
the following three services through a DT.
_1) Data Monitoring:_ Monitoring ACC-relevant data flowing through a vehicle is crucial for tracking system performance. The DT presented in this case will contain a user
interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate
reports of vehicle state data. Additionally, the UI will display
real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and
detected object tracks in the vehicle’s proximity.
_2) Computation Offloading:_ A DT can alleviate the computational burden placed on resource-constrained on-board
hardware by allocating compute to better-suited equipment,
e.g., in the cloud. In this case study, we demonstrate this
functionality by offloading only the ACC system from the
vehicle to the DT. The ACC system determines the lead
vehicle, then computes a desired speed based on the distance
to the lead vehicle, finally sending the desired speed back to
the vehicle where the actuation is carried out.


(a) Base hardware: teleoperated vehicle retrofitted with advanced
sensor and control technology



(b) Main hardware layers of the physical twin: lower and upper level
chassis, and the autonomy elements



Fig. 1: The physical twin: a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle



_3) Remote Management:_ The ability to remotely manage
ADAS systems enables the development, testing, and operation
of these systems from a different geographical location than
the physical twin. In addition to displaying real-time state data,
the UI will also enable the management and operation of ACC
remotely. This includes enabling/disabling ACC, setting the
desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command.
To implement these services, the DT must support low
end-to-end latency communication with the vehicle, contain
data collection and reporting capabilities, and more—all while
respecting the safety-critical nature of the system. For convenience and following community best practices, we refer
to the collection of the DT Entity, physical and middleware
components, and the UI as the digital twin system (DTS).
The users of our system can be grouped into two classes:
operators and developers. Operators use the DT to monitor
and remotely manage ACC, and developers use the DT to
troubleshoot during development using the data collection and
monitoring capabilities. In the _Liberty to act vs. Ability to act_
taxonomy from David and Bork [30], this DTS falls under the
Fully Autonomous Digital Twin, with the Human-in-the-Loop.


_B. Physical Twin_

coordination between devices. To enable computation
offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.


**Physical Twin Entity*** **Digital Twin Entity** **User Entity**
block_5_docs_retrieved:
In addition, a demonstration of the DT is publicly available. [2]


II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We

_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin

automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the

component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.

V. R EFLECTION AND C ONCLUSIONS


We reported on a case study of an automotive DT, through
which we investigate the fitness of the ISO 23247 standard
in engineering such systems. Our experiences highlight both
benefits and limitations of the standard in automotive settings.
On a positive note, we found the standard’s reference
architecture (RA) to be **a useful starting point for auto-**
**motive DTs** as it provides guidance for organizing blocks
of functionality. Additionally, we found the standard **easy to**
**use** as it does not assume substantial DT expertise and has a
comfortable learning curve. Our case was developed by two
graduate students, starting from a course project. [4] Through
this case study, we also highlight the **convenient generality**
of ISO 23247. The independence from specific format and
protocol requirements, as well as the ability to rely on domainspecific technologies in a straightforward manner, facilitated
the transfer of the RA to a new domain (automotive).
On a critical note, rigid adherence to the RA may lead to an
**under-utilization of resources** . In contrast to OMEs, vehicles
are resource-rich systems of their own. Thus, we recommend
promoting the physical twin to a first-class entity in automotive
DT architectures. This will allow for deploying FEs on the
vehicle, potentially in a flexible and dynamic fashion. For
example, our case study deploys the Data Pre-Processing FE
and the Actuation FE onto the physical twin, as opposed to
the DT Entity as prescribed by the ISO 23247 RA. Flexibility
in the deployment of FEs is at the heart of existing automotive
standards—e.g., AUTOSAR, the _de facto_ automotive software
architecture standard—where SW components are defined in a
hardware-agnostic manner, making it easier to retrofit existing
automotive architectures into automotive DT architectures.

Our case study further suggests that the RA is **insufficient for**
**safety-critical applications such as automotive** . Therefore,
we recommend safety to be promoted to a first-class citizen
in automotive DT standards by relying on existing automotive
safety standards—e.g., ISO 26262 [38].
Our work is a first look into the benefits and challenges of
adopting a standardized DT architecture for automotive DTs.
In order to aid the development of proper domain-specific
standards, we call for additional research into the topic, with
a particular emphasis on safety, security, data management,
and considering the regulated nature of the automotive domain, compatibility with existing standards. Additionally, we
encourage the development of automotive DT case studies and
exemplars to provide empirical evidence for further research.
In future work, we plan to research the safety aspects of
automotive DTs in more detail and elicit recommendations
